title: Jealousy
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Jealousy
summary: Scripture warns that jealousy rots the bones and fractures communities, urging believers to find contentment in God's unique purposes for their lives rather than coveting what others have.
body:
Jealousy is one of the oldest sins in Scripture. Cain's murder of Abel begins not with hatred but with envy — his offering is not regarded, his brother's is, and the resulting resentment festers into violence [verse:gen.4.4-8]. The pattern repeats with Joseph's brothers, with Saul's obsession with David, and with the religious leaders who hand Jesus over to Pilate.

Proverbs delivers the physiological metaphor: envy rots the bones [verse:prov.14.30]. Unlike anger, which burns hot and fast, jealousy is a slow corrosion. It does not destroy from the outside — it hollows a person from within. James connects envy to disorder: where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there is disorder and every vile practice [verse:jas.3.16].

Paul includes jealousy in his list of the works of the flesh [verse:gal.5.19-21] and contrasts it with love, which does not envy [verse:1cor.13.4]. The cure is not suppression but reorientation. When believers grasp that God has given each person distinct gifts and a unique calling [verse:rom.12.6], comparing becomes irrelevant. What God has for your neighbor is not taken from your portion.

The tenth commandment — do not covet — is not simply about behavior but about the heart's posture toward what belongs to others [verse:exod.20.17]. Freedom from jealousy begins when contentment takes root.

verses.annotation: Envy as internal rot — it does not simply affect mood but corrodes the whole person.
Jealousy and selfish ambition produce disorder and every kind of evil.
Cain's jealousy over Abel's accepted offering — the first murder rooted in envy.
Love's defining characteristic includes the absence of envy.
Jealousy listed among the works of the flesh opposed to the Spirit's fruit.
Diverse gifts given by grace — each person's calling is unique and incomparable.
The tenth commandment prohibits coveting, addressing the internal desire rather than just the external act.

scholars.quote: Davids argues that James 3:16 presents jealousy as socially destructive in a way that other sins are not — it fractures the community by turning gifts and blessings into sources of competition rather than mutual benefit.

Fee reads Paul's love chapter as a deliberate corrective to the Corinthian church's competitive spirit — envy was destroying the community, and Paul's response was to redefine greatness around self-giving love.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, 2014)

scholars.quote: Longman notes that Proverbs' somatic imagery — envy rotting the bones — conveys that jealousy is not merely an emotional problem but one that affects the whole person.
